[discussion] Discussion In this study, we found that offspring born to mothers with GDM had larger area and size of CC flow voids at 7 years of age, indicating that GDM might influence the choroidal microvasculature in offspring from early life on. Furthermore, offspring born to mothers with GDM had increased VD of the DVC in the parafoveal temporal quadrant compared with those born to mothers without GDM, which might be a compensation for the defected blood flow in the CC. Diabetic retinopathy (DR) is a common microvascular complication of diabetes. Previous studies using OCTA have found that diabetes will affect both retinal and choroidal vessels, resulting in significant changes in retinal and choroidal blood flow. 9 – 12 , 20 Because the choroid is the vascular layer that supplies the outer retina, choroidal blood flow has attracted more and more attention. Recent studies have found that the CC blood flow of DR patients is generally reduced compared with healthy people. In diabetic patients without DR, the decrease in CC blood flow is also obvious. 9 , 21 Therefore, these studies suggest that CC microvascular changes occur early in diabetes and may play a role in the pathogenesis of DR. It has been reported that the microvascular ischemia originated in the choroidal layer and extended inward, affecting the deep and superficial layers of retina. 11 CC flow voids have been used to assess CC blood perfusion, reflecting areas in the CC with low perfusion or without perfusion. The quantitative analysis of CC flow voids used in our study has shown good repeatability in previous studies. 19 , 22 – 24 Offspring born to mothers with GDM have been found to have a high risk of developing diabetes. 5 Our study found that the area and size of CC flow voids both increased in offspring exposed to maternal GDM, suggesting that these offspring might have already had reduced CC blood perfusion before they developed diabetes or DR. The reduced CC perfusion might serve as an early screening biomarker of diabetes or DR in GDM offspring. Moreover, maternal diabetes during pregnancy has been reported to be associated with an increased risk of myopia in offspring. 7 This association might be partially mediated by reduced CC perfusion in these offspring, which might impair scleral oxygen supply and subsequently trigger hypoxia-induced remodeling of the scleral extracellular matrix. Therefore, the reduced CC perfusion might also be a sign of developing myopia in GDM offspring. However, compared with previous studies, 9 , 25 the differences in the area and size of CC flow voids between the GDM and control groups seemed slight in our study. This might be due to the young age (7 years old) of the offspring in our study, who had not yet developed any organic diseases. In contrast, the study populations in previous studies have been about 40 to 60 years old, and they had already developed diabetes; some of them had even developed microaneurysms in the fundus, the earliest clinical sign of DR. We also found that offspring born to mothers with GDM had increased VD of the DVC in the parafoveal temporal quadrant, but there were no differences between the GDM and control groups in the VD of the DVC in other regions or in the VD of the SVC. Several studies found no significant differences in either the superficial or deep capillary plexuses between diabetic eyes without DR and healthy eyes. 9 , 26 , 27 However, other studies have found increased retinal blood flow in diabetic eyes without DR compared with healthy eyes. 28 – 30 Among them, a study reported that retinal blood velocity and flow were increased in diabetic eyes without DR but decreased in diabetic eyes with mild non-proliferative DR, compared with healthy age-matched controls. 28 This finding suggested an initial increase followed by a progressive decline in retinal blood flow as diabetic eyes progress from having no retinopathy to developing retinopathy, and the initial increase in retinal blood flow might be a compensation for the potential hypoxia in the retina. We also speculate that the increased VD of the DVC in the parafoveal temporal quadrant in GDM offspring found in our study might be a compensation for the decreased blood flow in CC to maintain oxygen supply to the outer retina. Animal studies have demonstrated that, under physiological conditions, choroidal circulation serves as the dominant oxygen source for the outer retina, whereas retinal circulation plays a negligible role; however, under systemic hypoxia, the retinal circulation increases oxygen delivery to the outer retina to compensate for reduced oxygen supply from the choroidal circulation. 31 – 33 In this study, we did not find any differences in macular thickness between the GDM and control groups. However, a previous study found that children from diabetic pregnancies had significantly thinner macular thickness compared with children from nondiabetic pregnancies. 8 This discrepancy might be due to two reasons. First, diabetic pregnancies are not the same as GDM pregnancies, as they also include chronic diabetes complicated by pregnancy, which might have more influence on the ocular health in offspring. Second, the offspring in our study were much younger than those in the previous study (age range, 11.1–14.4 years), so that an impact of GDM on macular thickness in offspring was not shown. The serum glucose from pregnant women is transported to the fetus through the placenta by facilitated diffusion. About half of the serum glucose is used by the placenta and the fetus, and the rest is excreted into the amniotic fluid via direct penetration or fetal urine. Maternal hyperglycemia can affect the glucose transporter expression and glucose transport activity in the placenta, causing an increase in glucose uptake. 34 , 35 The fetus exposed to high glucose levels in the uterus has increased secretion of insulin to compensate for the high glucose, which may cause corresponding metabolic disorders. 5 , 8 Chronic hyperglycemia impacts various pathways and leads to oxidative stresses, further causing mitochondrial dysfunction, dysfunction of inflammatory mediator, and hypoxia. Histopathological studies have observed that metabolic disorders caused by diabetes are related to variable choroidal abnormalities and mainly limited to the CC, including vascular dropout. 36 – 38 Zhang et al. 39 used chick embryos to establish a hyperglycemia model. The expression of Pax6 , Six3 , and Otx2 genes was inhibited in the model, inducing eye malformation. Although this result cannot be directly extrapolated to humans, we need to be vigilant about the possible impact of hyperglycemia on the development of children's eyes. 1 , 6 As far as we know, our research is the first study that evaluates the influence of GDM on microvascular changes in macular retina and CC in offspring using OCTA. We used quantitative parameters to analyze the influence of GDM on ocular microvasculature and minimized the artifacts as much as possible. Our research also has some limitations. First, the prevalence of GDM (6.0%) in our study was much lower than that reported in mainland China (14.8%), 2 which might be due to recall bias, as we collected the history of maternal GDM through a questionnaire. However, the prevalence of GDM varies substantially across different regions of mainland China. In Jiangsu Province, studies have reported a GDM prevalence ranging from 5.9% to 16.5%. 40 , 41 A meta-analysis from China also reported that the prevalence of GDM from 2012 to 2016 was significantly higher than that from 2005 to 2012, which might be related to the improvement of living standards. 42 Thus, the low prevalence of GDM in our study might also be due to the offspring in our study being born between 2011 and 2012. Second, we failed to collect blood test indexes of GDM, such as blood glucose level and glycosylated hemoglobin A1, which makes us unable to further explore the relationship between the severity of GDM and ocular microvascular changes in offspring. Third, as a cross-sectional study, we could not make any causal inference for the associations we found. Fourth, the CC flow voids we measured might be affected by the blood velocity in the CC, which may cause measurement error. Fifth, the FAZ parameters were derived from full-thickness retinal vasculature images, which precluded the separate analysis of the FAZ metrics for the SVC and DVC. Finally, our analysis of multiple retinal regions raises the issue of multiple comparisons, which increases the risk of type I error. However, higher VD in the DVC of the GDM offspring group compared to controls was consistently observed in many retinal regions. Although these differences might not reach statistical significance after adjusting for multiple comparisons, our findings indicate an underlying biological phenomenon worthy of further investigation in larger, confirmatory studies. More research is necessary to clarify the potential effect of GDM on ocular development in offspring.

[conclusion] Conclusions In this cross-sectional population-based study, we found that offspring born to mothers with GDM had larger area and size of CC flow voids, as well as increased VD of the DVC in the parafoveal temporal quadrant, compared with those born to mothers without GDM. These findings suggest that GDM has a potential effect on ocular microvasculature in offspring. Offspring exposed to maternal GDM might be recommended for more comprehensive eye examinations and intensive follow-up observations.

retrieved_papers.title: Exploration of choroidal vascular changes in juveniles with different degrees of myopia: The application of swept-source optical coherence tomography angiography.
retrieved_papers.body:
[Year: 2025]
[Abstract] To investigate the vascular characteristic changes in the macular retina and choriocapillaris, as well as the choroidal vascularization of eyes with different degrees of myopia in juveniles using Swept-source Optical Coherence Tomography (SS-OCT), aiming to understand the pathophysiological characteristics of myopic eyes further.
We conducted a retrospective case-control study involving 103 patients (198 eyes) aged 6-17 years who had undergone SS-OCT examination. Based on equivalent spherical power, they were divided into four groups: emmetropia group, low myopia group, moderate myopia group, and high myopia group. The density of superficial capillary plexuses (SCP), choriocapillaris (CC), choroid vessel (CV)layers, and subfoveal choroidal thickness (SFCT) in the macular area were measured using SS-OCT. Multivariate regression analysis was employed to determine the ocular and demographic factors associated with retinal and choroidal blood flow indicators.
In the temporal parafoveal area, the superficial capillary plexus (SCP) density was significantly lower in the moderate myopia group compared to the low myopia group (47.27 ± 2.21 vs. 48.51 ± 1.8, p = 0.002). Conversely, subfoveal choroidal vessel (CV) density was substantially higher in highly myopic eyes than in emmetropic eyes (56.98± 3.65 vs. 53.70 ± 4.31, p = 0.023). The multiple linear regression analysis showed that in the area under the fovea, thinner choroidal thickness is associated with higher choroidal vessel density. Specifically, as choroidal thickness decreases, the density of choroidal vessels increases (p = 0.024).
For juveniles, with the progression of myopia, the density of retinal superficial blood vessels and choroidal capillaries remains at a relatively stable level, while the choroidal vessel density under the fovea gradually increases. The main cause of changes in choroidal vessel density is the thickness of choroid under the fovea.

retrieved_papers.title: Comprehensive analysis of posterior segment characteristics in high myopic Turkish patients.
retrieved_papers.body:
[Year: 2025]
[Abstract] We aimed to investigate the structural characteristics of high myopia (HM) in a Turkish cohort using spectral domain optical coherence tomography (OCT) and evaluate the relationship between axial length (AL) and various retinal, choroidal, and scleral parameters.
A retrospective analysis of medical records and imaging data was conducted in HM patients examined at Dokuz Eylül University between January 2019 and January 2023. Eyes were categorized into three groups based on AL: Group 1 (26.00-27.49 mm), Group 2 (27.50-28.99 mm), and Group 3 (≥ 29.00 mm). Retinal, choroidal, and scleral parameters were quantitatively assessed, and posterior segment findings were analyzed across these groups.
A total of 146 eyes from 91 patients (mean age: 46.55 ± 17.05 years) were included. Central foveal thickness was significantly lower in Group 1 compared to Group 2 (p = 0.017). Moreover, Group 1 exhibited lower values for average macular thickness, average macular volume, and scleral sink, along with higher subfoveal choroidal thickness values compared to both Group 2 and Group 3 (p < 0.05 for all comparisons). The most common posterior segment findings were peripapillary atrophy (91.8%), tilted disc (47.3%), vitreomacular adhesion (39.0%), and posterior staphyloma (PS, 27.4%). Group 3 exhibited the most advanced stages of myopic maculopathy among the groups (p = 0.001). 'Plus' lesions, defined as the presence of macular neovascularization (MNV), Fuchs spots, or lacquer cracks, were detected in 14.4% of eyes. Pathologic myopia was diagnosed in 49.3% of eyes, including 47.2% based on myopic maculopathy stage and 0.7% each on the presence of PS, active MNV, or scarred MNV. Active MNV was more frequent in Group 3, while scarred MNV was predominant in Group 2.
Present findings highlight the progressive nature of structural changes associated with increasing AL in HM patients, emphasizing the importance of early detection and monitoring of posterior segment complications. This study provides valuable insights into the prevalence and distribution of myopia-related complications in a Turkish population, aiding in the clinical management of HM.

[Introduction] Background The rising prevalence of high myopia (HM) has emerged as a major public health concern over the past few decades [ 1 ]. Even though HM is commonly defined by a spherical equivalent (SE) refractive error of ≥ -6.00 diopters (D) and/or an axial length (AL) of ≥ 26.00 mm [ 2 ], it is still challenging to determine its prevalence due to variations in its definition. The global prevalence of HM is reported to be approximately 4% [ 1 ]. In Turkey, a study by Onal et al. [ 3 ] on medical students aged 18–26 reported a HM prevalence of 1.4% among myopic individuals and 0.4% in the overall population. Additionally, Kaya [ 4 ] reported a HM prevalence of 1.7% in Turkish myopic children. Pathologic myopia (PM), a more severe form of the condition, is characterized by structural alterations in the posterior segment, such as posterior staphyloma (PS), myopic maculopathy, and HM-associated optic neuropathy, which predominantly affect the posterior pole [ 5 ]. Although these pathological findings are frequently observed in eyes with HM, they can also be present in individuals with less severe refractive errors or normal AL values [ 6 ]. Axial elongation in HM is primarily driven by biomechanical stretching of the ocular tissues, resulting from progressive ocular growth. At the cellular level, axial elongation is associated with extracellular matrix remodeling within the sclera, characterized by decreased collagen synthesis and increased matrix metalloproteinase activity, which collectively reduce scleral rigidity. These alterations facilitate posterior scleral expansion, especially at the posterior pole, leading to thinning of the retina, choroid, and sclera [ 7 ]. The resulting structural changes contribute to a range of sight-threatening complications in HM, with the macular region being particularly susceptible. These complications can induce irreversible photoreceptor damage, ultimately leading to central vision loss [ 8 ]. The utilization of optical coherence tomography (OCT) has revolutionized the visualization and classification of HM-related posterior segment changes, offering near-histological resolution superior to conventional fundoscopy [ 2 , 9 ]. As both environmental and genetic factors are thought to contribute to the onset and progression of HM, region-specific investigations are crucial for identifying population-based variations. Despite the rising global prevalence of HM, there remains a notable lack of large-scale, imaging-based studies characterizing the posterior segment features of Turkish adults with HM. To address this gap, the present study aims to provide comprehensive multimodal imaging data from a Turkish cohort, thereby contributing to a deeper understanding of the structural alterations and their clinical implications in this population.

[introduction] Background The rising prevalence of high myopia (HM) has emerged as a major public health concern over the past few decades [ 1 ]. Even though HM is commonly defined by a spherical equivalent (SE) refractive error of ≥ -6.00 diopters (D) and/or an axial length (AL) of ≥ 26.00 mm [ 2 ], it is still challenging to determine its prevalence due to variations in its definition. The global prevalence of HM is reported to be approximately 4% [ 1 ]. In Turkey, a study by Onal et al. [ 3 ] on medical students aged 18–26 reported a HM prevalence of 1.4% among myopic individuals and 0.4% in the overall population. Additionally, Kaya [ 4 ] reported a HM prevalence of 1.7% in Turkish myopic children. Pathologic myopia (PM), a more severe form of the condition, is characterized by structural alterations in the posterior segment, such as posterior staphyloma (PS), myopic maculopathy, and HM-associated optic neuropathy, which predominantly affect the posterior pole [ 5 ]. Although these pathological findings are frequently observed in eyes with HM, they can also be present in individuals with less severe refractive errors or normal AL values [ 6 ]. Axial elongation in HM is primarily driven by biomechanical stretching of the ocular tissues, resulting from progressive ocular growth. At the cellular level, axial elongation is associated with extracellular matrix remodeling within the sclera, characterized by decreased collagen synthesis and increased matrix metalloproteinase activity, which collectively reduce scleral rigidity. These alterations facilitate posterior scleral expansion, especially at the posterior pole, leading to thinning of the retina, choroid, and sclera [ 7 ]. The resulting structural changes contribute to a range of sight-threatening complications in HM, with the macular region being particularly susceptible. These complications can induce irreversible photoreceptor damage, ultimately leading to central vision loss [ 8 ]. The utilization of optical coherence tomography (OCT) has revolutionized the visualization and classification of HM-related posterior segment changes, offering near-histological resolution superior to conventional fundoscopy [ 2 , 9 ]. As both environmental and genetic factors are thought to contribute to the onset and progression of HM, region-specific investigations are crucial for identifying population-based variations. Despite the rising global prevalence of HM, there remains a notable lack of large-scale, imaging-based studies characterizing the posterior segment features of Turkish adults with HM. To address this gap, the present study aims to provide comprehensive multimodal imaging data from a Turkish cohort, thereby contributing to a deeper understanding of the structural alterations and their clinical implications in this population.

[methods] Methods This retrospective cross-sectional study was conducted in accordance with the Declaration of Helsinki and local ethical regulations, with approval from the Dokuz Eylül University Non-Interventional Research Ethics Committee (Approval No: 2023/06–23). The medical records, ophthalmologic examination findings, and multimodal imaging data, including spectral-domain OCT (SD-OCT) and color fundus images (CFI), were reviewed for patients with HM who underwent examination at the Dokuz Eylül University Department of Ophthalmology between January 2019 and January 2023. Inclusion and exclusion criteria Patients were included in the study if they met the following criteria: AL ≥ 26.00 mm, age ≥ 18 years, and SD-OCT image quality ≥ 18. Exclusion criteria included age < 18 years, previous ocular surgery other than cataract surgery (e.g., refractive surgery, glaucoma surgery, or vitreoretinal surgery), SD-OCT image quality < 18, significant lens opacity (cataract grade ≥ 3 according to the Emery-Little classification) [ 10 ], ophthalmic diseases apart from HM, and a history of trauma or systemic medication use potentially affecting retinal or macular morphology. Demographic and clinical data Demographic and clinical data were extracted from medical records, including SE refractive error for phakic eyes, which was calculated as spherical refraction plus half of the cylindrical error. The Nidek TonoRef II (NIDEK Co., Ltd, Japan) was used for measurements. Best-corrected visual acuity (BCVA) was assessed using the Snellen chart and converted to logMAR for statistical analysis. The IOL Master 500 (Carl Zeiss Meditec, Jena, Germany) was used to measure AL, and lens status (phakic or pseudophakic) was determined via slit-lamp biomicroscopy. Optical coherence tomography imaging protocol Spectral-domain OCT imaging was performed using the Heidelberg Spectralis (Heidelberg Engineering, Heidelberg, Germany). A 7 × 7 mm macular scan centered on the fovea was acquired, consisting of 49 horizontal OCT sections spaced 160 μm apart. A similar 7 × 7 mm optic disc scan was obtained with 49 horizontal OCT sections at 160 μm intervals. In addition, a single 9-mm transfoveal horizontal enhanced-depth imaging (EDI) OCT scan was performed. Analysis of optical coherence tomography sections In the 7-mm single horizontal OCT section through the fovea (Fig. 1 A), several parameters were assessed. The central foveal thickness (CFT) was measured as the shortest distance between the internal limiting membrane (ILM) and Bruch’s membrane (BM) (Fig. 1 B) [ 11 ]. Scleral sink (SS) was measured as the vertical distance between the lowest and highest points of the retinal pigment epithelium (RPE) (Fig. 1 C) [ 12 ]. Mirror artifact (MA) was identified as image inversion caused by increased retinal curvature (Fig. 1 D) [ 12 ]. Dome-shaped maculopathy (DSM) was characterized by a protrusion of ≥ 50 μm from the lowest RPE level toward the vitreous (Fig. 1 E) [ 12 ]. Lamellar macular hole (LMH) was defined as partial separation of the inner retinal layers at the fovea (Fig. 1 F) [ 13 ], while full-thickness macular hole (FTMH) represented complete separation of retinal layers from ILM to RPE (Fig. 1 G) [ 14 ]. Myopic macular retinoschisis (MMR) referred to a separation of the foveal and/or parafoveal neuroretinal layers by a hyporeflective space (Fig. 1 H) [ 14 ]. Vitreomacular adhesion (VMA) was identified as partial vitreoretinal separation without distortion [ 15 ], and vitreomacular traction (VMT) was defined as partial vitreoretinal separation with associated retinal distortion [ 15 ]. Fig. 1 Evaluation of a single transfoveal horizontal 7-mm optical coherence tomography (OCT) section. A ; an example of a transfoveal 7-mm horizontal OCT section. B ; the measurement of central foveal thickness as the shortest distance between the internal limiting membrane (ILM) and Bruch’s membrane (a). C ; the measurement of scleral sink as the vertical distance between the lowest and highest points of the retinal pigment epithelium (RPE, b). D ; white arrow indicating the mirror artifact. E ; a protrusion of ≥ 50 μm from the lowest RPE level toward the vitreous (c), indicating dome-shaped macula. F ; partial separation of the inner retinal layers at the fovea indicating a lamellar macular hole (white arrow). G ; complete separation of retinal layers from ILM to RPE, indicating a full-thickness macular hole. H ; separation of the foveal neuroretinal layers by a hyporeflective space, indicating myopic macular retinoschisis Horizontal OCT sections obtained in a 7 × 7 mm area centered on the fovea were further analyzed for additional parameters (Fig. 2 A). The average thickness and volume values within the central 1-mm retinal area, as defined in the Early Treatment Diabetic Retinopathy Study, were recorded as average macular thickness (AMT) and average macular volume (AMV), respectively, using the retinal thickness map feature of the device (Fig. 2 B) [ 11 ]. Epiretinal membrane (ERM) was defined by the presence of an irregular hyperreflective band on the ILM [ 13 ]. Macular neovascularization (MNV) was characterized by a hyperreflective membrane on the RPE in the outer retinal layers [ 14 ]. Hemorrhage or intraretinal/subretinal fluid-associated MNVs were considered ‘active’ (Fig. 2 C), while MNVs without these findings, and Fuchs spots, were classified as ‘scarred’ (Fig. 2 D). Fig. 2 Evaluation of horizontal optical coherence tomography (OCT) sections obtained from a 7 × 7 mm area centered on the fovea and/or optic disc. A ; nine subfields according to the Early Treatment Diabetic Retinopathy Study (ETDRS). B ; average macular thickness (272 μm) and average macular volume (0.21 mm 3 ) within the central 1-mm retinal area based on ETDRS. C ; hyperreflective material above the retinal pigment epithelium (RPE) in association with intraretinal and subretinal fluid, indicating an active macular neovascularization (MNV). D ; hyperreflective material above the retinal pigment epithelium without hemorrhage or intraretinal/subretinal fluid, indicating a scarred MNV. E ; horizontal OCT sections from a 7 × 7 mm area centered on the optic disc. F ; a hyporeflective space within the inner choroidal layers beneath an intact RPE, indicating a peripapillary intrachoroidal cavitation. G ; the separation of inner retinal layers, characterized by a distinct hyporeflective space, indicating peripapillary retinoschisis. H ; cystic spaces or defects within the inner retinal layers in the perivascular areas, indicating perivascular inner retinal defect Horizontal OCT sections from a 7 × 7 mm area centered on the optic disc were analyzed for specific retinal abnormalities (Fig. 2 E). Peripapillary intrachoroidal cavitation (PICC) was diagnosed upon identifying a hyporeflective space within the inner choroidal layers beneath an intact RPE (Fig. 2 F) [ 16 ]. Peripapillary retinoschisis (PR) was defined by the separation of retinal layers, characterized by a distinct hyporeflective gap (Fig. 2 G) [ 14 ]. Horizontal OCT sections obtained in a 7 × 7 mm area centered on the fovea and/or optic disc, passing through areas adjacent to retinal vascular structures were evaluated for cystic spaces or defects within the inner retinal layers. These findings were classified as perivascular inner retinal defects (PIRDs) (Fig. 2 H) [ 17 ]. Analysis of enhanced-depth imaging optical coherence tomography sections In the 9-mm transfoveal horizontal EDI-OCT section, several key parameters were assessed (Fig. 3 A). Subfoveal choroidal thickness (SFCT) was defined as the shortest distance between the hyperreflective outer boundary of the RPE and the choroid-sclera junction (Fig. 3 B) [ 12 ]. Posterior staphyloma was identified by the presence of an outward scleral protrusion, with a curvature radius smaller than that of the adjacent sclera (Fig. 3 C) [ 18 ]. Subfoveal scleral visibility (SFSV) was measured as the distance between the choroid-sclera junction and the outermost discernible level of scleral reflectivity (Fig. 3 D) [ 12 ]. Fig. 3 Evaluation of a single transfoveal horizontal 9-mm enhanced depth imaging optical coherence tomography (EDI-OCT) section. A ; an example of a transfoveal 9-mm horizontal EDI-OCT section. B ; the measurement of subfoveal choroidal thickness as the shortest distance between the hyperreflective outer boundary of the retinal pigment epithelium and the choroid-sclera junction (a). C ; an outward scleral protrusion, with a curvature radius smaller than that of the adjacent sclera, indicating posterior staphyloma. D ; the measurement of subfoveal scleral visibility as the distance between the choroid-sclera junction and the outermost discernible level of scleral reflectivity (b) Color fundus imaging protocol and analysis Color fundus images were acquired using the Zeiss VISUCAM 500 (Carl Zeiss Meditec, Jena, Germany), covering a central 45-degree field that included the optic disc and macula. The Meta-Analysis for Pathologic Myopia (META-PM) classification was applied to categorize myopic maculopathy into five categories: category 0 (normal fundus), category 1 (tigroid fundus), category 2 (diffuse chorioretinal atrophy), category 3 (patchy chorioretinal atrophy), and category 4 (macular atrophy) [ 19 ]. The presence of additional posterior segment findings, including peripapillary atrophy (PPA), lacquer cracks (LC), tilted disc, and Fuchs spots were also evaluated. Group classification Eyes with active or scarred MNV / Fuchs spot or LC were classified as having ‘plus’ lesions. Eyes included in the study were classified as having PM if they exhibited myopic maculopathy equal or greater than category 2 or higher, and/or PS, and/or at least one ‘plus’ lesion. As all eyes included in the study had an axial length AL ≥ 26.00 mm, consistent with the definition of HM, they were further classified into three subgroups to assess the impact of varying degrees of axial elongation on structural findings. Specifically, Group 1 comprised eyes with AL ranging from 26.00 to 27.49 mm, Group 2 included eyes with AL between 27.50 and 28.99 mm, and Group 3 consisted of eyes with AL ≥ 29.00 mm. This stratification allowed for a more detailed evaluation of progressive anatomical alterations within the HM cohort. Statistical analysis The data collected in this study were analyzed using SPSS 28.0 (Statistical Package for the Social Sciences). Descriptive statistical methods, including frequency, percentage, mean, standard deviation, minimum, median, and maximum values, were employed to summarize the data. The normality of the data distribution was assessed using the Kolmogorov-Smirnov and Shapiro-Wilk tests. Parametric tests were applied to measurements that followed a normal distribution, while non-parametric tests were utilized for data that did not conform to normality. For quantitative data, analysis of variance (ANOVA) was performed to compare the means of at least three independent groups with normally distributed data, while the Kruskal-Wallis test was used to compare the medians of non-normally distributed data across independent groups. For the post-hoc analyses, independent samples t-tests and Mann-Whitney U tests with Bonferroni correction were used for pairwise comparisons. The relationship between categorical variables was assessed using the Chi-square test, and Fisher’s Exact test was employed when the expected frequencies for any category were less than five. A p -value below 0.05 was considered statistically significant.

[results] Results A total of 146 eyes from 91 patients were included in the study. The cohort comprised of 37 males (40.7%) and 54 females (59.3%), with a mean age of 46.55 ± 17.05 years (range: 18–84 years). Both eyes of 55 patients (60.4%) met the inclusion criteria. Of the 146 eyes included in the study, 123 (84.2%) were phakic, and 23 (15.8%) were pseudophakic. Group 1 comprised 79 eyes from 54 patients, while Group 2 included 32 eyes from 25 patients, and Group 3 included 35 eyes from 23 patients. No significant differences were observed between the groups in terms of sex distribution ( p = 0.864) or mean age ( p = 0.067). However, a significantly higher percentage of phakic eyes was observed in Group 1 compared to the other groups ( p = 0.001). Statistically significant differences were found between the three groups in terms of the mean SE value ( p = 0.001), AL ( p < 0.001), and BCVA ( p < 0.001), as expected (Table 1 ). Notably, AMT and AMV could not be calculated by the device for one eye in Group 2 and six eyes in Group 3. Post-hoc analyses revealed that the significant difference in CFT was primarily driven by a higher value in Group 2 compared to Group 1 ( p = 0.017). The differences in AMT, AMV, and SS were attributed to Group 1 exhibiting lower values compared to Group 2 and Group 3. Specifically, for AMT, Group 1 showed significantly lower values than both Group 2 and Group 3 ( p = 0.026 and p = 0.049, respectively). Similar trends were observed for AMV, with Group 1 having lower values than Group 2 and Group 3 ( p = 0.019 and p = 0.049, respectively). For SS, Group 1 also had significantly lower values compared to both Group 2 and Group 3 ( p < 0.001 for both). A significant difference in mean SCFT was observed across all pairwise group comparisons, with Group 1 displaying the highest thickness and Group 3 the lowest ( p = 0.016 for Group 1 vs. Group 2, p < 0.001 for Group 1 vs. Group 3, and p < 0.001 for Group 2 vs. Group 3). No significant difference was observed among the three groups in terms of the mean SFSV ( p = 0.354) (Table 2 ). Significant differences were found among the groups regarding the presence of PPA, tilted disc, VMA, PS, MA, ERM, PR, PIRD, DSM, LC, scarred MNV, active MNV, and MMR ( p < 0.05 for all). All these findings were more frequently observed in Group 3, except for VMA, which was more common in Group 1, and scarred MNV, which was more prevalent in Group 2. No significant differences were observed among the groups in terms of the presence of PICC, LMH, FTMH, and VMT ( p > 0.05 for all). Among the eyes with LMH, one was included in Group 1, while two were in Group 3. Additionally, one eye with FTMH and another with VMT were classified in Group 3.
